intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

body.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

draft.title: recipe app for people who hate cooking
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
recipe app for people who hate cooking

Building your first draft…

data.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

facade.hypothesis: Users want visual confirmation of what they're cooking with, not ingredient lists
facade.label: Ingredient photos
facade.acceptImplies: Recipe app should lead with high-quality food photography and ingredient imagery to reduce cooking anxiety through recognition
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer compact, scannable ingredient lists (text/numbers) over visual presentation — efficiency over reassurance

evidence.hypothesis: Users want visual confirmation of what they're cooking with, not ingredient lists
evidence.implication: Recipe app should lead with high-quality food photography and ingredient imagery to reduce cooking anxiety through recognition

facade.hypothesis: The app suggests ingredients as you type, with smooth dropdown animations and instant visual feedback—reducing friction for users who don't know what they have on hand.
facade.axisTargeted: fluid responsiveness vs manual search burden
facade.label: Ingredient autocomplete
facade.acceptImplies: Users value speed and scaffolding over blank-slate control. Automate the tedious parts.
facade.rejectImplies: Users want to browse or think through options themselves. Autocomplete feels pushy or prescriptive for a hate-cooking audience.

facade.hypothesis: A recipe app for cooking-haters needs to feel low-pressure and forgiving—timers that don't punish you for being 2 minutes late, notifications that are gentle nudges rather than alarms.
facade.label: Forgiving timer
facade.acceptImplies: The app should prioritize reassurance and flexibility over precision and urgency. Users want permission to be casual.
facade.rejectImplies: Users want clear accountability and structured guidance—they need the app to keep them on track, not let them off the hook.

facade.hypothesis: A recipe app for people who hate cooking should feel approachable and low-pressure—soft, rounded UI elements signal 'this is easy, relax' vs sharp corners which suggest precision or intimidation
facade.label: Playful rounded corners
facade.acceptImplies: Design system favors curves, bouncy micro-interactions, and friendly padding—reduces cognitive load, appeals to casual/reluctant cooks
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers cleaner minimalism or structured precision—suggests app should feel efficient/no-nonsense, or that softness alone doesn't build trust for recipe-following

facade.hypothesis: Users want a voice that's witty and irreverent—gently mocking the cooking process itself rather than patronizing them for hating it.
facade.label: Snarky little robot
facade.acceptImplies: The app's copy, notifications, and error messages should lean playful and self-aware ('Yeah, we know you'd rather order takeout'). Builds affinity through humor.
facade.rejectImplies: Users want genuine support and straightforward help, not comedy. Voice should be warm but sincere—validating their frustration without performing it.

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking want minimal friction—a single button that transforms the interface into a streamlined, instruction-focused view that hides all non-essential options.
facade.label: One-tap cooking mode
facade.acceptImplies: The app should support modal/toggle states that drastically simplify the UI when actively cooking, suggesting users value task-focused layouts over persistent navigation.
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer a consistent, always-available interface structure even while cooking, suggesting they want to browse or context-switch freely without mode-switching friction.

axes.label: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
Whether user wants minimal decision-making (curated picks) vs. maximum filtering control (dietary, time, skill filters)
Whether user prefers instant gratification (no loading, snappy) vs. exploratory browsing with smooth transitions and discovery

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants minimal decision-making (curated picks) vs. maximum filtering control (dietary, time, skill filters)
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers instant gratification (no loading, snappy) vs. exploratory browsing with smooth transitions and discovery
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users anxious about cooking want real-time guidance and reassurance, not just instructions—a visible timer reduces uncertainty and decision paralysis during cooking steps
facade.axisTargeted: Information Design: procedural guidance as temporal scaffolding vs. static recipe text
facade.label: Timer with countdown
facade.acceptImplies: Recipe app should embed timers, progress indicators, and step-by-step pacing cues directly into the interface to externalize cooking anxiety
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer self-directed pacing and don't need real-time pressure/structure; app should focus on ingredient prep and setup, letting cooks move at their own speed

draft.title: QuickBite – Recipe App for People Who Hate Cooking
draft.summary: A recipe app that strips away complexity and delivers only the fastest, easiest meals with minimal ingredients and steps. Perfect for busy people who want food without the fuss.
draft.html:
QuickBite
Meals in 15 minutes or less. No excuses.
Filter by mood
Today's picks
Pasta aglio e olio
⏱ 8 min
🛒 4 items
Avocado toast
⏱ 5 min
🛒 3 items
Tuna salad wrap
⏱ 7 min
🛒 5 items
